text: Welcome to This is My Architecture. I'm Morit. Joining me today, Tomer from Funbox.
Hi Tomer, tell us about Funbox. So Funbox is a startup company. We are using AI and
machine learning to help our customers manage their credit lines. What are we going to talk
about today? Today we are going to describe a backend platform we built in the data organization.
This platform orchestrates data jobs. What was the challenge that you wanted to solve?
Prior to this platform we had a different set of EC2 instances in AWS. And as a user I
had to SSH into one of those EC2 instances, upload my files whether it's a SQL file or
Python script or anything else to manage everything by myself and hope that my job will execute on time
and nothing fails. That was challenging and this is why we decided to shift a bit and to separate the
business logic from the underneath infrastructure and to help user progress in more faster fashion.
Let's dive into the flow. As a BI developer or machine learning engineer or data engineer,
I have a file that describes my job. It could be a Python script, a SQL script, a Spark job or anything.
Along with this file that describes my code, I need to create another file. This is a configuration file
in which I need to define the type of the job I have. If it's a SQL, so SQL. If it's a Python,
a Python and so on. Those two files are committed into a dedicated GitHub repository. GitHub then
triggers a Jenkins job that will take those files, wrap them in a Docker container, upload the Docker container
directly to ECR, create an ECS Fargate task for me and create a step function for the job. The step function
needs to manage all the infrastructure and decides when to execute the job. From then on, I have a few options to run my job.
I can just type a Slack command that sends an HTTP request to API Gateway and API Gateway will trigger
manager lambda. I can wait for a CRON job, a CRON expression to kick in and then do the same, just trigger the
manager lambda. The manager lambda will pick all the job configurations from a database and will trigger
the step function for me. The step function will do all the Docker related things. We'll grab the ECR
ECR repository and run the ECS task on Fargate and will eventually send an SNS notification describing the
job status, whether it failed or succeeded. The SNS message then fanned out to a set of Lambda functions.
The first Lambda function is an audit function that takes the job result and write it to an S3 bucket.
Another Lambda function is a Slack Lambda function that eventually sends notification to the user's
channel in Slack and tells the user if his job failed or not. And another job, another microservice,
is a dependency Lambda. This dependency Lambda decides if we need to execute more jobs downstream. I mean,
if my job, I need two triggers and other jobs that I configured. So this dependency Lambda will decide
and then send back the event to the manager Lambda and then all the flow runs again.
So this dependency Lambda is a dependency Lambda. Where is the data? Does the data sit? So data can reside on few data sources. We have our warehouse or S3 bucket or external APIs.
So the code needs to interact with the warehouse or S3. So the code needs to interact with the warehouse or S3 through a set of APIs that we provide to the end users.
How do you manage the permissions of the users to the data?
How do you manage the data? Okay. So inside GitHub, we have a structure of directories that every job role in the company need to put these files inside a specific directory.
Jenkins then decides according to this directory which IAM roles and which permissions to grant to this user. The IAM roles are shared with the Farget task and the step function.
And this is what limits the user to access our warehouse or data lake.
So you actually separate the data access from the users?
Yes. They are in the jobs. They don't need to care about infrastructure. They don't need to care about permissions that Job Orchestrator actually runs their job.
Exactly. All the interaction they have is with GitHub or via Slack. They also get logs through Slack. They can browse the history of the job through Slack.
So as a user, all I have to do is to have my Slack open and GitHub commit and push commands on my hands.
Sounds fun. How does the platform manages different volumes of jobs?
So because we are leveraging serverless technologies, we don't care of what happens down underneath of our jobs. I mean, if we have in a point in time, one job or hundreds of jobs, it doesn't matter.
The platform runs smoothly. We depend on ECS Fargate and Lambdas. This can scale very easily.
So basically we reside on serverless technologies.
Who are the users of the platform?

As I mentioned, users might be machine learning engineers. They can write jobs like data extraction. If I'm a data engineer that uses the platform, I can write my ETL jobs. If I'm a BI developer analyst, I'm using SQL to describe my jobs. These are the set of users we are serving today.
What type of jobs does the platform support?
The platform supports actually any type of job because we are using Docker containers. We can compress into the Docker container any kind of language or runtime we want.
Today we're actively support SQL and Python, but users can also provide a Docker container, Docker file for themselves and describe any runtime they want, such as the server.

They can also describe any runtime they want, such as Node.js code or a JavaScript or anything that they want to execute. And the system will treat this job as if it was a normal job. Wrap it in a container and let the platform execute this job for me.
I assume they are happy users. Do you have any future plans for the platform?

Definitely. First, we want to create some console, I mean a UI interface that will help users to understand what is the status of their job if there are other jobs that need to execute as a consequence.

Another feature we want to add is automatically resource tuning. I mean, if my job failed for resources, I mean, I didn't allocate enough memory to the job. So we want the platform to manage that automatically to tune the Fargate task automatically for me.
Thanks, Domen for sharing that. And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
Thank you.
